_note: Anna PV-focused translation set — 8 short B1 prompts targeting her ★★★★★ tier from progress/phrasal-verbs-tracker-anna.md (find out, pick up, take out, turn off, put on, get up, hurry up, look for). Anna's v1 prep set proved short prompts (3-6 words RU → 3-7 words EN) work; this batch follows that. Common errors target Russian L1 calques specific to each PV. Article-stripping in s90r2 normalize() means a/an/the variants don't need to be enumerated.

exercises.source_observation: ★★★★★ in Anna's tracker, never tested in production. Russian 'узнать' calques to 'know' — drill the find_out form.

common_errors.feedback: Russian 'узнать' covers both 'find out' (discover) and 'know' (have knowledge). For 'Узнаю завтра' = I'll discover the answer tomorrow, use the PV: ✓ 'I'll find out tomorrow'. ✗ 'I will know tomorrow' = I'll have the knowledge tomorrow (different meaning, also unnatural).

✗ Missing subject — Russian 'Узнаю завтра' drops the subject 'я'. English requires it: ✓ 'I'll find out tomorrow' (or 'I will find out tomorrow'). Even short imperatives keep the subject in declarative sentences.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'll find out tomorrow.' Russian 'узнать' = find out (discover) — use the PV, don't substitute 'know'. And keep the subject 'I'.

exercises.source_observation: ★★★★★ in Anna's tracker — daily family logistics. Russian 'забрать' often calques to 'take from'.
correct_answers: Will you pick up the kids from school
Can you pick up the kids from school
Will you pick the kids up from school
Will you pick up the children from school
Can you pick up the children from school

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Take the kids from school' — direct calque of Russian 'забрать из'. English idiom for collecting people/things from a place: ✓ 'pick up the kids'. The PV 'pick up' carries the meaning of collecting from a location.
'Get the kids from school' is understandable but not the natural choice. ✓ 'pick up the kids from school' is the standard PV for this scenario.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'Will you pick up the kids from school?' Russian 'забрать' = collect from a place → 'pick up' (not 'take from').

exercises.source_observation: ★★★★★ in Anna's tracker — daily home life. Russian 'вынести' (carry out) is close to literal — drill the PV form.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Carry out the trash' — direct calque of Russian 'вынести'. 'Carry out' in English means 'execute / perform' (carry out a plan, carry out research). For removing trash, use ✓ 'take out the trash'.
✗ 'Take the trash' is incomplete — needs the particle. ✓ 'Take out the trash' or 'take the trash out' (separable PV).

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'Take out the trash, please.' The PV is 'take out' (separable). Russian 'вынести' ≠ 'carry out' — that's the trap.

exercises.source_observation: ★★★★★ in Anna's tracker. Russian 'выключить' sometimes calques to 'close' (closing a circuit metaphor). Drill the standard 'turn off' / 'switch off'.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Close the light' — Russian L1 calque (выключить ≈ closing a circuit). English doesn't say this. ✓ 'Turn off the light' or 'switch off the light'. 'Close' in English is for doors, windows, books, accounts — not devices.
✗ 'Off the light' is incomplete — English needs the verb. ✓ 'Turn off' or 'switch off' the light.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'Turn off the light' or 'Switch off the light.' Russian 'выключить' = turn off / switch off — never 'close' for devices.

exercises.source_observation: ★★★★★ in Anna's tracker — clothes/makeup daily. 'Wear' vs 'put on' is the action/state distinction Russian doesn't mark.
correct_answers: Put on a jacket, it's cold
Put on the jacket, it's cold
Put a jacket on, it's cold
Put on a jacket, it is cold

common_errors.feedback: 'Wear a jacket' = the state of having it on (e.g. 'I wear a jacket every day'). 'Надень' is an action — about to put it on right now. ✓ 'Put on a jacket'. The PV signals the action of dressing.
✗ 'Dress in a jacket' is overly formal/awkward. Use the everyday PV: ✓ 'Put on a jacket'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'Put on a jacket, it's cold.' For the *action* of dressing → 'put on'. For the *state* of being dressed → 'wear'. Russian 'надеть' = the action.

exercises.source_observation: ★★★★★ A1 foundation. Pair-confusion with 'wake up' — wake up = stop sleeping, get up = leave the bed.
correct_answers: I get up at seven o'clock

common_errors.pattern_label: wake up for get up (pair confusion)
common_errors.feedback: 'Wake up' = stop sleeping (you can wake up and stay in bed). 'Get up' = leave the bed and start the day. Russian 'вставать' = get up (the action of getting out of bed). ✓ 'I get up at seven'.
'Rise at seven' is correct but very formal/literary. Everyday: ✓ 'I get up at seven'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I get up at seven.' 'Get up' = leave the bed (action). 'Wake up' = stop sleeping (different — you can wake up and stay in bed).

exercises.source_observation: ★★★★★ — family/kids urgency. Russian 'торопиться' tends to drop the 'up' in English.
correct_answers: Hurry up, we're going to be late

common_errors.feedback: 'Hurry, we're late' is OK but the more natural everyday form is the PV: ✓ 'Hurry up, we're late!' The particle 'up' adds the urgency / call-to-action that matches Russian 'поторопись'.
✗ 'Be fast' is a Russian L1 calque (быстрее). English idiom: ✓ 'Hurry up'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'Hurry up, we're late!' The PV is 'hurry up' — keep the particle 'up' for the urgency.

exercises.source_observation: ★★★★★ — most common Anna PV. The look_for ↔ look_after pair confusion is well-documented; this drill targets the search direction.
correct_answers: I am looking for my glasses
I am looking for the glasses

common_errors.pattern_label: look after (caregiving) instead of look for
common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'Look after my glasses' = take care of them (caregiving). For 'искать' = search → ✓ 'looking FOR my glasses'. The pair is a particle-swap trap: same base 'look', opposite meanings.
✗ 'Looking my glasses' — 'look' as a search verb needs 'for'. ✓ 'looking FOR my glasses'.
'Search' takes 'for' too: 'searching FOR my glasses'. Target PV here is 'look for' — practise that one.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'm looking for my glasses.' Russian 'искать' always maps to 'look FOR' — never 'look after' (that's caregiving).
